nodes.title: Research and program staff with aquaculture and animal welfare expertise
nodes.text: Interdisciplinary team combining marine biology, animal welfare science, development economics, and field implementation experience

connections.evidence: FWI has conducted multiple field studies in India, China, and Philippines. Team has published peer-reviewed research and established academic partnerships.
connections.assumptions: Research staff can be retained and expertise developed. Field research access maintained in key countries.

connections.evidence: Track record of publishing in peer-reviewed journals. Academic collaborations established with universities.
connections.assumptions: Research quality meets publication standards. Academic journals receptive to fish welfare research.

connections.evidence: Currently managing partnerships with 155+ farms through ARA program. Demonstrated farmer engagement and retention.
connections.assumptions: Staff capacity sufficient for program management. Farmer relationships maintained over time.

nodes.title: $830K annual budget for research, programs, and operations
nodes.text: Funding for field research, farmer outreach, technology development, policy engagement, and organizational operations across multiple countries

connections.evidence: 2024 budget of $590K supported research activities in India and China. 2025 budget increased to $830K indicating donor confidence.
connections.assumptions: Funding sustained over multi-year projects. Donors continue supporting fish welfare as cause area.

connections.evidence: ARA program operational costs supported by current funding. Cost per fish helped (~$0.14) suggests financial sustainability.
connections.assumptions: Program costs don't increase faster than funding. Farmer partnership model remains cost-effective.

connections.evidence: FWI launched Stunning RFP and Satellite Imagery Challenge in 2024, demonstrating capacity to fund innovation initiatives.
connections.assumptions: Innovation challenges produce usable results. Additional funding available for technology development.

nodes.title: Relationships with aquaculture farmers and producer associations
nodes.text: Direct partnerships with fish farmers in India, emerging relationships in China and Philippines, connections to industry associations and cooperatives

connections.evidence: 155+ active farmers in ARA program. High retention rates and farmer satisfaction scores. Word-of-mouth recruitment working.
connections.assumptions: Farmer trust maintained through beneficial outcomes. Network effects continue driving recruitment.

connections.evidence: Established relationships with key stakeholders in Indian aquaculture. Growing network in China through program expansion.
connections.assumptions: Stakeholder relationships translate to broader influence. Industry associations receptive to welfare messaging.

nodes.title: Collaborations with technology developers and research institutions
nodes.text: Partnerships for developing welfare monitoring technologies, satellite imagery analysis, water quality sensors, and innovation competitions

connections.evidence: Launched innovation challenges but results still emerging. Some technology partnerships established but early stage.
connections.assumptions: Technology partnerships produce viable solutions. External innovators interested in fish welfare applications.

connections.evidence: Developing welfare assessment methods through research partnerships. Water quality and stocking density protocols established.
connections.assumptions: Technology partnerships enhance protocol development. Assessment methods can be standardized across contexts.

nodes.title: Field research documenting welfare problems and testing intervention effectiveness
nodes.text: Controlled studies measuring welfare impacts of water quality improvements, stocking density changes, and handling practices across different farming contexts

connections.evidence: Field studies in India have documented welfare problems and intervention effectiveness. Research methodology established and validated.
connections.assumptions: Field research access maintained. Study designs capture meaningful welfare indicators.

connections.evidence: ARA program based on field-tested interventions. Water quality and stocking density improvements proven effective in practice.
connections.assumptions: Research findings translate to practical applications. Solutions remain effective across different contexts.

nodes.title: Peer-reviewed research establishing fish sentience and welfare science
nodes.text: Publications documenting fish consciousness, suffering capacity, welfare assessment methods, and intervention effectiveness in academic journals

connections.evidence: FWI researchers have publication track record. Fish sentience research gaining academic acceptance. Journals publishing fish welfare studies.
connections.assumptions: Publication pipeline continues. Academic community receptive to fish welfare research.

nodes.title: Alliance for Responsible Aquaculture partnerships with farmers implementing welfare standards
nodes.text: Direct program helping 155+ farms improve water quality and reduce stocking density, with regular monitoring and technical assistance

connections.evidence: ARA program currently helping ~1.2 million fish with verified welfare improvements. Cost-effective at ~7 fish per dollar.
connections.assumptions: Welfare improvements are genuine and sustained. Monitoring accurately captures impact.

connections.evidence: ARA farmers reporting economic benefits including better survival rates, reduced disease, improved feed conversion ratios.
connections.assumptions: Economic benefits persist over time. Early adopters influence peer farmers.

nodes.title: Standardized welfare assessment protocols and implementation guidelines
nodes.text: Evidence-based methods for measuring fish welfare, practical guidelines for improvement interventions, training materials for farmers and industry

connections.evidence: Water quality and stocking density protocols developed through field research. Methods tested across multiple farm contexts.
connections.assumptions: Protocols capture meaningful welfare differences. Methods can be standardized across regions.

connections.evidence: Training materials developed for ARA farmers. Educational content created for broader stakeholder engagement.
connections.assumptions: Educational materials effectively communicate welfare concepts. Stakeholders receptive to training.

nodes.title: Evidence-based policy recommendations for fish welfare regulations
nodes.text: Policy briefs, regulatory frameworks, and government engagement promoting minimum welfare standards for aquaculture

connections.evidence: Limited progress on fish welfare regulation globally. EU developing some standards. Most countries lack specific fish welfare laws.
connections.assumptions: Policymakers receptive to welfare arguments. Industry won't successfully oppose regulations.

nodes.title: Industry conferences, workshops, and educational campaigns
nodes.text: Outreach to farmers, processors, retailers, and consumers about fish welfare issues and solutions

connections.evidence: Stakeholder engagement activities conducted but impact measurement limited. Industry conferences provide platform for welfare messaging.
connections.assumptions: Stakeholders attend and engage meaningfully. Educational messaging changes attitudes and behavior.

connections.evidence: Consumer awareness of fish welfare very low compared to other animal welfare issues. Limited retailer engagement to date.
connections.assumptions: Market actors respond to welfare messaging. Consumer demand can be developed for welfare-certified products.

nodes.title: Technology innovation competitions and solution development
nodes.text: Stunning RFP, Satellite Imagery Innovation Challenge, and other initiatives crowdsourcing technological solutions for welfare problems

connections.evidence: Innovation challenges launched in 2024 but results still emerging. Some promising technology concepts identified.
connections.assumptions: Innovation competitions produce viable solutions. Technologies can be scaled cost-effectively.

nodes.title: Fish welfare recognized as legitimate scientific and ethical concern
nodes.text: Academic acceptance of fish sentience research, inclusion in animal welfare curricula, recognition by scientific bodies

connections.evidence: Growing academic acceptance of fish sentience. Some universities adding fish welfare to curricula. Scientific conferences including fish welfare sessions.
connections.assumptions: Scientific legitimacy translates to broader stakeholder acceptance. Academic recognition influences policy and industry.

nodes.title: Comprehensive scientific evidence documents welfare problems and solutions
nodes.text: Research base establishing specific welfare issues in aquaculture and demonstrating effectiveness of improvement interventions

connections.evidence: FWI and others building evidence base documenting welfare problems. Intervention studies showing measurable improvements.
connections.assumptions: Evidence base reaches sufficient comprehensiveness. Research quality meets scientific standards.

nodes.title: Field research demonstrates feasible welfare improvement methods
nodes.text: Proven interventions for water quality, stocking density, handling, and other welfare issues validated across farming contexts

connections.evidence: ARA program demonstrating practical solutions. Farmers reporting economic benefits from welfare improvements.
connections.assumptions: Solutions remain effective when scaled. Economic benefits persist across different market conditions.

nodes.title: Welfare improvements proven cost-effective and economically beneficial
nodes.text: Demonstrated that welfare improvement practices maintain or improve farmer profitability through better survival, growth, and feed conversion

connections.evidence: ARA farmers reporting economic benefits including 43% better survival rates with water quality improvements, improved feed conversion ratios.
connections.assumptions: Economic benefits persist over time and scale. Cost-benefit analysis remains positive across different contexts.

nodes.title: Key stakeholders educated about fish welfare issues and solutions
nodes.text: Farmers, industry, policymakers, and consumers understand fish welfare problems and available improvement methods

connections.evidence: Some industry stakeholder engagement but limited behavior change documented. Educational campaigns reaching target audiences.
connections.assumptions: Education translates to attitude and behavior change. Stakeholders have capacity to implement improvements.

nodes.title: Practical welfare assessment methods developed and validated
nodes.text: Standardized, cost-effective methods for measuring fish welfare that can be implemented by farmers, auditors, and regulators

connections.evidence: Water quality and stocking density assessment methods proven effective. Some industry adoption of welfare measurement approaches.
connections.assumptions: Assessment methods capture meaningful welfare differences. Industry accepts standardized measurement approaches.

nodes.title: Industry champions demonstrate welfare improvements are viable
nodes.text: Leading farmers and companies successfully implementing welfare practices, serving as models and advocates for broader adoption

connections.evidence: ARA farmers serving as examples of welfare implementation. Some larger producers beginning to adopt welfare practices.
connections.assumptions: Early adopter success influences peer adoption. Champions actively advocate for welfare improvements.

nodes.title: Welfare monitoring and improvement technologies are accessible and affordable
nodes.text: Cost-effective technological solutions for welfare assessment, water quality monitoring, and other improvement methods available to farmers

connections.evidence: Some technology solutions emerging from innovation challenges. Water quality monitoring becoming more affordable.
connections.assumptions: Technologies can be scaled cost-effectively. Farmers adopt technological solutions when accessible.

nodes.title: Fish welfare becomes recognized priority within aquaculture industry
nodes.text: Industry associations, major producers, and supply chain actors acknowledge welfare importance and begin implementing improvements

connections.evidence: Some industry recognition emerging but limited systematic adoption. ASC and other certification schemes beginning to include welfare elements.
connections.assumptions: Industry recognition translates to concrete adoption. Welfare becomes competitive advantage rather than cost burden.

connections.evidence: Limited consumer awareness of fish welfare compared to other animal welfare issues. Some retailer commitments beginning to emerge.
connections.assumptions: Industry recognition influences market positioning. Consumer demand develops for welfare-certified products.

nodes.title: Government regulations mandate minimum welfare standards
nodes.text: Policy development and implementation of fish welfare regulations in key aquaculture-producing countries

connections.evidence: EU developing fish welfare regulations. Limited progress in other major producing countries. Industry typically resists new regulations initially.
connections.assumptions: Regulations are implemented and enforced effectively. Industry complies rather than lobbying for exemptions or delays.

nodes.title: Consumer and corporate demand drives welfare-certified products
nodes.text: Market mechanisms create economic incentives for welfare improvements through consumer preferences and corporate commitments

connections.evidence: Consumer awareness of fish welfare very low. Limited retailer commitments. Price sensitivity high for fish products.
connections.assumptions: Consumer education campaigns succeed. Retailers make meaningful welfare commitments. Price premiums for welfare products acceptable.

nodes.title: Welfare improvement technologies and practices are widely accessible
nodes.text: Cost-effective, practical solutions for welfare improvements available to farmers across different contexts and scales

connections.evidence: ARA program proving welfare improvements possible at farm scale. Technology costs decreasing for water quality monitoring.
connections.assumptions: Solutions maintain effectiveness when scaled. Technology adoption barriers overcome by cost reduction and usability improvements.

nodes.title: Major aquaculture producers adopt welfare standards benefiting 100 million fish annually
nodes.text: Widespread implementation of water quality, stocking density, and handling improvements across industrial aquaculture systems by 2030

connections.evidence: Large-scale aquaculture operations can affect millions of fish per facility. China alone produces 65% of global farmed fish.
connections.assumptions: Producer adoption reaches sufficient scale. Welfare improvements are genuine and sustained. Impact measurement accurately captures benefits.

nodes.title: Direct intervention programs scale to help millions of fish annually
nodes.text: Expansion of Alliance for Responsible Aquaculture and similar direct implementation programs to reach scale contributing to 100 million fish target

connections.evidence: ARA program currently helping ~1.2 million fish with proven welfare improvements. Scalable model demonstrated.
connections.assumptions: Direct programs can scale 100x current level. Staff capacity and funding sufficient for expansion. Farmer recruitment sustainable.

nodes.title: World where welfare of all sentient beings is recognized and protected
nodes.text: Aquatic animals receive same ethical consideration as terrestrial farm animals, with fish welfare integrated into broader animal protection movement
